title: Bracket Text Objects
subtitle: {key:i(} {key:i{} {key:i[} {key:i<} — operate on the contents of any bracket pair.
summary: i( / a( for parens, i{ / a{ for braces, i[ / a[ for square brackets, i< / a< for angle brackets. ib and iB are aliases for parens and braces.

blocks.text: Bracket objects are the most-used text objects in real code. They're how you yank arguments, change function bodies, and delete array literals without ever counting characters.

blocks.label: Delete a brace block including the braces

blocks.text: {key:i}{key:b} = {key:i}{key:(}. {key:i}{key:B} = {key:i}{key:{}. The letter aliases exist because some keyboards make `(` and `{` painful to type repeatedly. Pick whichever you prefer.

blocks.title: How Bracket Matching Works
blocks.text:
Bracket objects walk *outward* from the cursor looking for an unbalanced opener of the requested type. They respect nesting — {key:i}{key:(} on the inner of `f(g(x))` selects `g(x)`; from outside the inner parens it selects `g(x)` if you're inside the outer pair.

Matching uses the `'matchpairs'` option (default `(:),{:},[:],<:>`). It does **not** understand language syntax — strings and comments don't shield brackets, which is occasionally surprising in code with mismatched brackets in comments.

Bracket objects are *charwise*. Even {key:i}{key:{} on a multi-line block selects character-by-character — meaning the leading and trailing newlines are part of the range. That's usually what you want for change/delete, but it surprises people the first time {key:y}{key:i}{key:{} pastes back as a multi-line span starting in the middle of a line.

Pair these with the grammar's other halves: {key:>}{key:i}{key:{} indents inside braces, {key:=}{key:a}{key:(} re-indents the entire parenthesised expression, {key:g}{key:U}{key:i}{key:[} uppercases everything inside the brackets.
